docs.title: portapkg
docs.text:
Portable, offline-friendly Python package bundler and installer.

Bundle a package and all its dependencies on an online machine, then install on an air-gapped machine with nothing but Python and pip.

docs.title: Why portapkg?
docs.text:
• No server needed — Bundles are plain folders. Copy them on a USB drive and you're done.
  • Multi-platform — One bundle works across Windows, Linux, macOS, x86, and ARM.
  • Self-contained — The standalone portapkg.py is stdlib only — zero dependencies.
  • Export & ship — One command creates a portable folder with everything you need.

docs.title: On an online machine
docs.text:
pip install portapkg
portapkg bundle instrumation

This creates a bundles/ folder with wheels for all 6 platforms × 5 Python versions.

docs.title: On an offline machine
docs.text:
Copy bundles/ and portapkg.py to a USB drive, then run:

python portapkg.py list
python portapkg.py install instrumation

docs.title: Export as a portable package
docs.text:
portapkg export instrumation

Creates instrumation_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/ with portapkg.py + bundles/ inside — ready to ship.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Dependency resolution — pip download <package> resolves the full transitive dependency tree using pip's own resolver.
  2. Multi-platform fetch — For each dependency, wheels are downloaded across 6 platforms × 5 Python versions.
  3. Fallback — If no binary wheel exists for a platform, it falls back to a source distribution with a warning.
  4. Offline install — pip install --no-index --find-links <wheels_dir> installs from local files only.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Usage guide — detailed CLI and standalone instructions
  • CLI reference — full command reference
  • Export bundles — package everything into one portable folder
  • Configuration — environment variables and options
  • Troubleshooting — common issues and solutions
  • Development — contributing, testing, project structure

docs.title: Bug fixes
docs.text:
• BUNDLES_DIR resolution: Now uses os.getcwd() + PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR env var instead of working relative to __file__, so it works correctly regardless of how the tool is invoked.
  • macOS platform matching: ARM64 (arm64) vs x86_64 (x86_64) are now properly distinguished — no longer matches both architectures.
  • Source dist fallback: No longer uses --no-deps when falling back to source distributions. Keeps --platform and --python-version for correct fallback behavior.
  • Wheel deduplication: _wheel_exists() now checks compatibility against the requested platform before skipping — a win_amd64 wheel won't cause a linux_x86_64 download to be skipped.
  • Wheel filename parsing: Uses proper {name}-{version}-{tags} structure for all wheel parsing functions.

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• --packages flag: Bundle or update multiple packages in one command with portapkg bundle --packages pkg1,pkg2,pkg3.
  • export command: New portapkg export packages bundles + standalone installer into a portable folder with a timestamped name.
  • --name flag for export: Custom folder name for exported bundles.
  • Multi-package export: Export multiple bundles into a single folder with portapkg export --name mydev --packages pkg1,pkg2.
  • Documentation site: Full MkDocs site with usage guide, CLI reference, FAQ, troubleshooting, and development docs.
  • Blue theme: MkDocs-material documentation with slate scheme and blue/light-blue accent colors.
  • Documented PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR: Environment variable is now documented in README and docs.

docs.title: v0.1.0 (2026-05-22)
docs.text:
Initial release.

  • bundle — multi-platform package bundling
  • list — show all bundles
  • info — show bundle details
  • install — offline installation via pip install --no-index
  • update — re-fetch a bundle
  • Standalone portapkg.py — zero-dependency installer
  • 6 platforms × 5 Python versions support
  • Snapshot mode for single-platform bundling

docs.title: portapkg
docs.text: The main CLI for bundling packages on an online machine.

docs.title: portapkg bundle [package]
docs.text:
Bundle one or more packages for offline install.

# Single package
portapkg bundle instrumation

# Multiple packages at once
portapkg bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py

# With specific platforms
portapkg bundle instrumation --platforms win_amd64,macosx_13_0_arm64

# Snapshot mode
portapkg bundle instrumation --snapshot

Options:

Flag Description package Package name (positional, optional if --packages used) --packages Comma-separated list of packages to bundle --platforms Comma-separated platform tags (default: all 6) --snapshot Snapshot current environment (single-platform)

docs.title: portapkg list
docs.text:
List all bundles in the bundles directory.

portapkg list

docs.title: portapkg info <package>
docs.text:
Show details about a specific bundle.

portapkg info instrumation

docs.title: portapkg export [package]
docs.text:
Export one or more bundles + standalone installer into a portable folder.

# Single package (positional)
portapkg export instrumation

# Multiple packages with custom name
portapkg export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py

# Auto-generated name for multiple packages
portapkg export --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# To a specific directory
portapkg export instrumation --output ./dist

Creates {name}_{random_id}_{date}/ with portapkg.py + bundles/{package(s)}/ inside — ready to ship.

Options:

Flag Description package Package name (positional, optional if --packages used) --name Custom export folder name (default: package name or "bundle") --packages Comma-separated list of packages to include --output, -o Output directory (default: current directory)

docs.title: portapkg update [package]
docs.text:
Re-fetch one or more bundles (same options as bundle).

# Single package
portapkg update instrumation

# Multiple packages at once
portapkg update --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# With specific platforms
portapkg update instrumation --platforms win_amd64

# Snapshot mode
portapkg update instrumation --snapshot

Options:

Flag Description package Package name (positional, optional if --packages used) --packages Comma-separated list of packages to update --platforms Comma-separated platform tags --snapshot Snapshot mode

docs.title: Global options
docs.text: • PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR environment variable overrides the bundle directory

docs.title: portapkg.py
docs.text: The standalone script — works for both bundling and installing on any machine with Python and pip. No pip install portapkg needed.

docs.title: python portapkg.py bundle [package]
docs.text:
Bundle one or more packages (same options as the CLI).

# Single package
python portapkg.py bundle instrumation

# Multiple packages at once
python portapkg.py bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# With specific platforms
python portapkg.py bundle instrumation --platforms win_amd64,macosx_13_0_arm64

# Snapshot mode
python portapkg.py bundle instrumation --snapshot

docs.title: python portapkg.py export [package]
docs.text:
Export one or more bundles into a portable folder.

# Single package
python portapkg.py export instrumation

# Multiple packages with custom name
python portapkg.py export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# To a specific directory
python portapkg.py export instrumation --output ./dist

docs.title: python portapkg.py install <package>
docs.text:
Install a bundled package.

python portapkg.py install instrumation
python portapkg.py install instrumation --target ./venv/Lib/site-packages

Options:

Flag Description package Package name (positional, required) --target Custom install path

docs.title: python portapkg.py list
docs.text:
List available bundles.

python portapkg.py list

docs.title: Global options
docs.text: • PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR environment variable overrides the bundle directory

docs.title: PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR
docs.text:
Overrides the default bundle storage directory.

Default: ./bundles/ (relative to the current working directory)

Example:

export PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR=/data/portapkg/bundles
portapkg bundle instrumation
# stores in /data/portapkg/bundles/instrumation/

Both the CLI (portapkg) and the standalone script (portapkg.py) respect this environment variable.

docs.title: Default platforms and Python versions
docs.text:
When no --platforms flag is given, the bundler uses all 6 platforms:

Platform Tag Windows x86_64 win_amd64 Windows x86 win32 Linux x86_64 manylinux2014_x86_64 Linux ARM64 manylinux2014_aarch64 macOS Apple Silicon macosx_13_0_arm64 macOS Intel macosx_13_0_x86_64

And all 5 Python versions: 3.9, 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13

docs.title: Bundle structure
docs.text:
A typical bundle looks like this:

bundles/
└── instrumation/
    ├── manifest.json
    └── wheels/
        ├── instrumation-0.3.0-py3-none-any.whl
        ├── pyserial-3.5-py2.py3-none-any.whl
        └── ...

docs.title: manifest.json
docs.text:
Contains metadata about the bundle:

{
  "name": "instrumation",
  "version": "0.3.0",
  "date_bundled": "2026-05-26T12:00:00",
  "source_platform": "macosx_13_0_arm64",
  "source_python": "314",
  "dependencies": [
    {"name": "instrumation", "version": "0.3.0"},
    {"name": "pyserial", "version": "3.5"}
  ]
}

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
portapkg/
├── portapkg.py                ← standalone offline installer (stdlib only)
├── portapkg/
│   ├── __init__.py            ← version
│   ├── cli.py                 ← entrypoint: bundle/list/info/update
│   ├── bundler/
│   │   ├── fetch.py           ← multi-platform wheel download
│   │   ├── manifest.py        ← manifest.json read/write
│   │   └── resolver.py        ← dependency resolution
│   └── installer/
│       ├── install.py         ← offline install logic
│       └── platform.py        ← OS/arch/python detection
├── tests/                     ← pytest test suite
├── docs/                      ← documentation site (MkDocs)
├── pyproject.toml             ← packaging & tool config
└── .github/workflows/         ← CI / release / docs workflows

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/abduznik/portapkg-py
cd portapkg-py

# Install in editable mode with dev dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Install docs dependencies (optional)
pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material

docs.title: Running tests
docs.text:
# Run all tests
pytest

# With coverage
pytest --cov=portapkg --cov-report=term-missing

# Run a specific test file
pytest tests/test_platform.py

# Run a specific test
pytest tests/test_platform.py::TestParseWheelFilename

docs.title: Code style
docs.text:
This project uses Ruff for linting.

pip install ruff
ruff check portapkg/ tests/ portapkg.py

docs.title: Building documentation
docs.text:
# Preview locally
mkdocs serve

# Build static site
mkdocs build

The documentation is automatically deployed to GitHub Pages on push to main via .github/workflows/docs.yml.

docs.title: Making a release
docs.text:
1. Bump the version in portapkg/__init__.py and pyproject.toml
  2. Commit and tag: git tag v0.x.x && git push --tags
  3. Trigger the Publish workflow on GitHub, specifying the version tag

The workflow builds wheels + sdist, publishes to PyPI, and creates a GitHub Release with the artifacts attached.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
• Stdlib only — no third-party imports anywhere in the runtime code
  • Single-file fallback — portapkg.py must work with nothing but Python and pip
  • Pip as engine — all heavy lifting (download, resolve, install) delegates to pip; portapkg just orchestrates
  • Clear errors — every failure mode has a human-readable message with a suggested fix

docs.title: Exporting bundles
docs.text: The export command packages one or more bundles together with the standalone installer into a single portable folder — ready to copy to a USB drive and ship.

docs.title: Why export?
docs.text:
Instead of manually copying portapkg.py and the bundles/ directory, export creates a tidy, timestamped folder that includes everything the offline machine needs:

mydev_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/
├── portapkg.py          ← standalone installer (stdlib only)
└── bundles/
    ├── pyserial/
    │   ├── manifest.json
    │   └── wheels/
    └── pyvisa/
        ├── manifest.json
        └── wheels/

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: You must have already bundled the packages before exporting them.

docs.title: Export a single package
docs.text:
portapkg export pyserial
# Creates: pyserial_abc123_2026-05-26/

docs.title: Export multiple packages under a custom name
docs.text:
portapkg export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py
# Creates: mydev_abc123_2026-05-26/

docs.title: Export multiple packages with auto-generated name
docs.text:
portapkg export --packages pyserial,pyvisa
# Creates: bundle_abc123_2026-05-26/

docs.title: Export to a specific directory
docs.text: portapkg export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa --output ./dist

docs.title: How the folder is named
docs.text:
The export folder is named automatically as:

{name}_{short_id}_{today_date}
Part Description Example name The export name (--name, package, or "bundle") mydev short_id A random 6-character hex string a3f2e6 today_date Today's date in ISO format 2026-05-26

The name comes from (in order of priority):

  1. --name flag (always used if provided)
  2. Single package name (if exporting one package)
  3. "bundle" (fallback for multi-package exports without --name)

docs.title: What's inside
docs.text: File / folder Purpose portapkg.py The standalone offline installer — no dependencies, stdlib only bundles/{package}/ Each package's bundle manifest and wheels bundles/{package}/manifest.json Metadata (version, deps, platform info) bundles/{package}/wheels/ All downloaded wheel files

docs.title: Using the exported folder on an offline machine
docs.text:
# Copy the entire folder to the offline machine, then:

cd mydev_abc123_2026-05-26/

# List available bundles
python portapkg.py list

# Install a package (no internet needed)
python portapkg.py install pyserial
python portapkg.py install pyvisa

docs.title: Using portapkg.py from source as an alternative
docs.text:
If you don't want to pip install portapkg on the online machine, you can use the portapkg.py file directly from the source repository:

# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/abduznik/portapkg-py
cd portapkg-py

# Bundle and export without pip install
python3 portapkg.py bundle pyserial
python3 portapkg.py bundle pyvisa
python3 portapkg.py export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa

No install required

The portapkg.py script is fully self-contained. Use it for bundling (online), exporting, and installing (offline).

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text: • PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR — override the bundle directory (default: ./bundles/)

docs.title: What is portapkg for?
docs.text: Portapkg helps you install Python packages on machines that don't have internet access (air-gapped, classified, or strictly controlled environments). You bundle the packages on an online machine, transfer them via USB drive, then install offline.

docs.title: How is this different from pip download?
docs.text:
pip download <package> downloads wheels for your current platform only. Portapkg downloads for 6 platforms × 5 Python versions at once, so a single bundle works on Windows, Linux, macOS, x86, and ARM.

Portapkg also gives you:

  • A standalone portapkg.py installer (stdlib only, no dependencies)
  • An export command that packages everything into one portable folder
  • A manifest.json with metadata about the bundle

docs.title: Do I need to install portapkg on the offline machine?
docs.text: No. The standalone portapkg.py file has zero dependencies — it only uses the Python standard library and subprocess calls to pip. Copy it alongside the bundles/ folder and you're ready to install.

docs.title: Can I bundle for only one platform?
docs.text: Yes. Use --snapshot to bundle for your current machine only, or --platforms win_amd64 to target a single specific platform.

docs.title: What if a wheel doesn't exist for my platform?
docs.text: The bundler falls back to a source distribution (sdist). However, the sdist may require a C compiler (gcc, clang, or MSVC) to build on the target machine. If possible, prefer binary wheels by bundling on a machine with the same architecture as the target.

docs.title: Can I install to a virtual environment?
docs.text:
Yes. Use the --target flag:

python portapkg.py install pyserial --target ./venv/Lib/site-packages

Or activate the virtual environment first and install normally — pip will install into the active environment.

docs.title: Does portapkg work with conda?
docs.text: No. Portapkg uses pip under the hood, so it only works with PyPI packages. For conda environments, you can still use portapkg.py after activating the conda environment — pip will install into the current environment.

docs.title: What Python versions are supported?
docs.text:
• Bundling: Python 3.9+
  • Installing: Python 3.9+ (any machine with pip)
  • Bundled wheel coverage: Python 3.9 through 3.13 (all 5 versions)

docs.title: Are the bundles compressed or encrypted?
docs.text: No. Bundles are plain folders with standard .whl files inside. You can inspect them, modify them, or add additional wheels manually. Zip the folder to compress for transfer.

docs.title: What about packages with native extensions?
docs.text: Packages like numpy, pandas, or cryptography have compiled native code and publish platform-specific wheels (e.g., numpy-1.26.0-cp312-cp312-win_amd64.whl). The bundler downloads the correct wheel for each platform automatically.

docs.title: Can I bundle packages that aren't on PyPI?
docs.text: No. The bundler uses pip to resolve and download from PyPI. For private packages, you'd need to add them manually to the wheels/ directory and update the manifest.json.

docs.title: How do I update a bundle?
docs.text:
portapkg update pyserial

This re-fetches all wheels for the bundle with the same options as bundle. If the package has a newer version on PyPI, it'll be downloaded.

docs.title: Can I bundle multiple packages together in one folder?
docs.text:
Yes, use the --name flag with export:

portapkg bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa
portapkg export --name mydev --packages pyserial,pyvisa

This creates a folder mydev_abc123_2026-05-26/ with both bundles and the standalone installer.

docs.title: Real-world example
docs.text: This walkthrough shows a complete workflow: a developer needs to install Python instrumentation packages (pyvisa, pyserial, pyvisa-py) and a GUI app (flet) on an air-gapped Windows machine in a lab environment.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
• Online machine: macOS (Apple Silicon) — has internet
  • Offline machine: Windows 10 (x86_64) — no internet, no PyPI access
  • Goal: Get pyvisa, pyserial, pyvisa-py, and flet onto the Windows machine with all dependencies

docs.title: Step 1 — Bundle on the online machine
docs.text:
# Install portapkg (one-time)
pip install portapkg

# Bundle all 4 packages at once
portapkg bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py,flet

This downloads wheels for 6 platforms × 5 Python versions per dependency. Output in ./bundles/:

bundles/
├── pyserial/          (1 dep,   1 wheel)
├── pyvisa/            (2 deps,  2 wheels)
├── pyvisa-py/         (3 deps,  3 wheels)
└── flet/              (11 deps, 16 wheels)

Multi-platform means one bundle serves all machines

Even though we bundled on macOS, the downloaded wheels include win_amd64 variants — so the same bundle folder works on Windows.

docs.title: Step 2 — Export to a portable folder
docs.text:
portapkg export --name lab-pc --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py,flet

Creates:

lab-pc_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/
├── portapkg.py
└── bundles/
    ├── pyserial/
    ├── pyvisa/
    ├── pyvisa-py/
    └── flet/

Now zip the folder and copy it to a USB drive.

docs.title: Step 3 — Transfer via USB
docs.text:
# On the online machine — zip it up
zip -r lab-pc_a3f2e6_2026-05-26.zip lab-pc_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/

# Copy to USB drive, walk over to the Windows machine

docs.title: Step 4 — Install on the offline Windows machine
docs.text:
# Copy the zip onto the Windows machine and unzip
# Open PowerShell or Command Prompt in the extracted folder

cd lab-pc_a3f2e6_2026-05-26

# List what's available
python portapkg.py list

# Output:
#   pyserial   v3.5   (2026-05-26)
#   pyvisa     v1.16.2 (2026-05-26)
#   pyvisa-py  v0.8.1 (2026-05-26)
#   flet       v0.85.2 (2026-05-26)

# Install everything
python portapkg.py install pyserial
python portapkg.py install pyvisa
python portapkg.py install pyvisa-py
python portapkg.py install flet

No internet needed

Each install command runs pip install --no-index --find-links <wheels> — pip installs from the local .whl files. Zero network traffic.

docs.title: Alternative: Install into a virtual environment
docs.text:
# Create and activate a venv
python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate

# Install from the local bundles
python portapkg.py install pyvisa
python portapkg.py install flet

docs.title: Another scenario: Shipping a single-file tool
docs.text:
You have a Python script scan.py that depends on pyserial and pyvisa. You want to hand it to a colleague who has no internet.

# On your machine:
portapkg bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa
portapkg export --name scan-tool --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# Now the folder scan-tool_abc123_2026-05-26/ has everything.
# Drop your script in there:

cp scan.py scan-tool_abc123_2026-05-26/

Hand them the folder on a USB stick:

cd scan-tool_abc123_2026-05-26
python portapkg.py install pyserial
python portapkg.py install pyvisa
python scan.py

docs.title: What to watch out for
docs.text: Issue How to handle Package has native extensions Bundle on a machine with the same OS architecture as the target, or use --snapshot Very large bundles (100+ MB) Use --platforms win_amd64 to target only Windows, skipping macOS/Linux wheels Offline machine has old Python Check the bundled Python versions — default covers 3.9–3.13 pip not on the offline machine Run python -m ensurepip --upgrade first Need conda-compatible install Activate the conda environment first, then use portapkg.py install — pip installs into the active env

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Online machine                        USB stick                  Offline machine
───────────────                      ──────────                  ───────────────
pip install portapkg                                              
portapkg bundle --packages ...       bundles/                    python portapkg.py list
portapkg export --name lab-pc ...    lab-pc_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/   python portapkg.py install pyvisa
zip -r lab-pc.zip lab-pc_*/          lab-pc.zip → USB            python scan.py

docs.title: Standalone script (portapkg.py)
docs.text: The portapkg.py file is a fully self-contained offline installer that uses only the Python standard library (plus pip via subprocess). It has zero third-party dependencies.

docs.title: Why a standalone script?
docs.text:
• No pip install needed — just copy the file and run it
  • Works everywhere — any machine with Python 3.9+ and pip
  • Single file — easy to distribute, audit, or modify
  • Same bundle format — compatible with bundles created by the full CLI

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
# Copy portapkg.py alongside your bundles/ folder
# Then on the offline machine:

# List available bundles
python portapkg.py list

# Install a bundled package
python portapkg.py install instrumation

# Install to a custom path
python portapkg.py install instrumation --target ./venv/Lib/site-packages

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Reads the bundle's manifest.json to understand the package structure
  2. Scans the wheels/ directory for wheels compatible with the current platform and Python version
  3. Runs pip install --no-index --find-links <wheels_dir> <package> to install from local files only

docs.title: Platform detection
docs.text:
The script detects the current OS and CPU architecture to select compatible wheels:

Machine Platform reported Windows x86_64 win_amd64 Windows x86 win32 Linux x86_64 linux_x86_64 Linux ARM64 linux_aarch64 macOS Apple Silicon macosx_13_0_arm64 macOS Intel macosx_13_0_x86_64

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text: • PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR — override the bundle directory (default: ./bundles/)

docs.title: "No compatible wheel found"
docs.text:
This means the bundle doesn't contain a wheel that matches your current OS, architecture, or Python version.

Solutions:

  • Bundle with --snapshot on a machine with the same OS/Python as the target
  • Bundle with all default platforms (don't use --platforms filter on a platform the target machine doesn't match)
  • Check the target machine's Python version: python --version — make sure it's one of the Python versions you bundled for (default: 3.9–3.13)

docs.title: "No manifest in ..."
docs.text:
The bundle directory is missing a manifest.json file.

Solutions:

  • Delete the incomplete bundle and re-bundle
  • Make sure the bundle completed without errors (check for "Bundle saved" message)

docs.title: "pip is not available"
docs.text:
The standalone portapkg.py uses pip under the hood for installation, but pip isn't installed on the target machine.

Solutions:

  • Install pip: python -m ensurepip --upgrade
  • On some minimal Linux distributions, install the python3-pip package via the system package manager

docs.title: Multi-platform download warnings
docs.text:
When bundling, you may see warnings like:

pyvisa==1.16.2: no wheel available for win_amd64/39

This means the package doesn't publish wheels for that specific platform/Python combination. A source distribution (sdist) is used instead, which requires a C compiler on the target machine.

Solutions:

  • These warnings are usually safe to ignore — pip will fall back to the sdist
  • If installation fails on the target machine, try bundling with --snapshot on a machine identical to the target

docs.title: Large bundle sizes
docs.text:
Bundles with many dependencies (like auto-py-to-exe or flet) can be large — up to hundreds of megabytes when bundling for all 6 platforms.

Solutions:

  • Use --platforms to target only the platforms you need
  • Use --snapshot to bundle for just your current machine
  • Only bundle the specific packages you actually need, and skip heavy dev dependencies

docs.title: "No bundles found" after copying
docs.text:
You copied the bundles/ folder to the offline machine, but list shows nothing.

Solutions:

  • Make sure the bundles/ folder is in the same directory as portapkg.py
  • Or set PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR to point to the correct location
  • Verify the folder structure looks like: 
    bundles/
└── yourpackage/
    ├── manifest.json
    └── wheels/

docs.title: Permission errors on Linux/macOS
docs.text:
The portapkg.py file needs execute permission to run directly.

Solutions:

  • Run with Python: python3 portapkg.py install <package>
  • Or make it executable: chmod +x portapkg.py

docs.title: Installation fails on the target machine
docs.text:
The pip install step fails even though compatible wheels exist.

Solutions:

  • Make sure pip is up to date: python -m pip install --upgrade pip
  • Check for missing system dependencies (e.g., libffi-dev for cffi-based packages)
  • Try installing with verbose output: python -m pip install --no-index --find-links ./wheels <package> --verbose

docs.title: From PyPI (recommended)
docs.text: pip install portapkg

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/abduznik/portapkg-py
cd portapkg-py
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: No install needed — use portapkg.py directly
docs.text:
The portapkg.py file is fully self-contained — stdlib only, zero dependencies. You can use it for both bundling and installing without running pip install:

# Bundle a package (works if pip is available)
python3 portapkg.py bundle instrumation

# List bundles
python3 portapkg.py list

# Show bundle details  
python3 portapkg.py info instrumation

# Export for distribution
python3 portapkg.py export instrumation

One file to rule them all

portapkg.py works on both the online machine (bundling) and the offline machine (installing). Just copy it alongside your bundles/ folder and you're ready.

docs.title: Basic bundling
docs.text:
# Single package
portapkg bundle instrumation

# Multiple packages at once
portapkg bundle --packages pyserial,pyvisa,pyvisa-py

Single package downloads wheels for 6 platforms × 5 Python versions (up to 30 downloads per dependency). Multi-package bundles each one in sequence. The output goes to ./bundles/{package}/.

docs.title: Specific platforms
docs.text:
portapkg bundle pyserial --platforms win_amd64,macosx_13_0_arm64

Valid platform tags:

Platform Tag Windows x86_64 win_amd64 Windows x86 win32 Linux x86_64 manylinux2014_x86_64 Linux ARM64 manylinux2014_aarch64 macOS Apple Silicon macosx_13_0_arm64 macOS Intel macosx_13_0_x86_64

docs.title: Snapshot mode (single platform)
docs.text:
portapkg bundle instrumation --snapshot

Downloads wheels for your current machine only. Faster and more reliable, but the bundle only works on machines with the same OS and architecture.

docs.title: Custom bundle directory
docs.text: PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR=/path/to/bundles portapkg bundle instrumation

docs.title: List and inspect bundles
docs.text:
# List all bundles
portapkg list

# Show details of a specific bundle
portapkg info instrumation

docs.title: Update bundles
docs.text:
# Re-fetch a single bundle with all platforms
portapkg update instrumation

# Re-fetch multiple bundles at once
portapkg update --packages pyserial,pyvisa

# Re-fetch with specific platforms
portapkg update instrumation --platforms win_amd64,linux_x86_64

# Re-fetch as snapshot
portapkg update instrumation --snapshot

docs.title: Export a bundle for distribution
docs.text:
# Export the bundle + portapkg.py into a portable folder
portapkg export instrumation

# Export to a specific directory
portapkg export instrumation --output ./dist

This creates a timestamped folder like instrumation_a3f2e6_2026-05-26/ containing portapkg.py and the bundles/ folder — ready to zip up or copy to a USB drive.

docs.title: Installing offline
docs.text: Copy the bundles/ folder and portapkg.py to the offline machine.

docs.title: List available bundles
docs.text: python portapkg.py list

docs.title: Install a bundled package
docs.text: python portapkg.py install instrumation

docs.title: Install to a custom path
docs.text: python portapkg.py install instrumation --target ./venv/Lib/site-packages

docs.title: Custom bundle directory
docs.text:
PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR=/path/to/bundles python portapkg.py list
PORTAPKG_BUNDLES_DIR=/path/to/bundles python portapkg.py install instrumation

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text: Online machine USB stick Offline machine portapkg bundle pyserial bundles/ folder python portapkg.py list portapkg bundle pyvisa portapkg.py file python portapkg.py install pyserial portapkg export pyserial pkg_abc123_2026-05-26/ folder unzip → python portapkg.py install pyserial → bundles ready in ./bundles/ Copy both → installs from local wheels

docs.title: Using portapkg.py without pip install
docs.text:
You can use the standalone portapkg.py from the source repository directly for both bundling and installing — no pip install required:

git clone https://github.com/abduznik/portapkg-py
cd portapkg-py

# Bundle a package (needs pip available)
python3 portapkg.py bundle instrumation

# Export for distribution
python3 portapkg.py export instrumation

# On the offline machine:
python3 portapkg.py install instrumation

portapkg.py is fully self-contained (stdlib only). The bundle and export commands delegate to pip under the hood, so they need pip available on the machine — but no portapkg-specific dependencies are needed.
